text: The Japanese practice of shinrin-yoku — forest bathing — entered public health policy in 1982 when the Japanese Forestry Agency promoted woodland walks as preventive medicine. Subsequent research by immunologist Qing Li demonstrated a specific mechanism: phytoncides, volatile organic compounds released by trees, increase natural killer cell activity in the immune system. Participants who spent three days in a cedar forest showed NK cell counts elevated for more than thirty days afterward. Li's work reframes nature exposure as an immunological event, not merely a psychological one. The forest communicates with the body's immune surveillance system through chemistry, at concentrations far below conscious smell.
question: What mechanism did Qing Li identify for the immune benefits of forest bathing?

options.text: Reduced cortisol allows natural killer cells to recover their depleted function
Negative air ions in forests directly stimulate lymphocyte production
Phytoncides — volatile compounds released by trees — increase natural killer cell activity
Physical activity in forest terrain produces anti-inflammatory cytokines
